---
title: Getting Started
engine: julia
---

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
using Terse
```

## Standalone Abstract Type

Use `@types` with just a name to declare an abstract type:

```{julia}
@types Organism
isabstracttype(Organism)
```

Parametric abstract types are supported too:

```{julia}
@types Container{T}
isabstracttype(Container)
```

## Abstract Type with Subtypes

Use `>` to define an abstract type and its concrete subtypes together:

```{julia}
@types Animal > (
    Cat(lives::Int),
    Dog(name::String)
)

supertype(Cat), supertype(Dog)
```

```{julia}
Cat(9), Dog("Rex")
```

## Parametric Types

Type parameters and bounds work as normal Julia syntax inside `{}`:

```{julia}
@types Envelope{T} > (
    Plain{T}(value::T),
    Labelled{T, S <: AbstractString}(value::T, label::S)
)

Labelled{Int, String}(42, "answer")
```

## Nested Hierarchies

A subtype can itself be an abstract type with its own subtypes by using `>` again.
Bare names (no parentheses) produce zero-field structs that inherit the parent's type parameters:

```{julia}
@types Animal2{T} > (
    Mammal{T}(family::T),
    Invertebrate{T} > (
        Worm,
        Insect{T, I <: Integer}(legs::I, family::T)
    )
)

supertype(Invertebrate{Float64}), supertype(Worm{Float64})
```

```{julia}
Worm{String}(), Insect{Float64, Int8}(6, 1.0)
```

## Single Concrete Type

Define a single struct without a hierarchy:

```{julia}
@types Point(x::Float64, y::Float64)
Point(1.0, 2.0)
```

To specify a supertype, use `<:`:

```{julia}
@types Organism2
@types Bacterium(name::String) <: Organism2

supertype(Bacterium)
```
